summary:
The Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt (1250–1517 AD) was a military state ruled by former slave-soldiers (mamluks — a class of purchased Turkish, Circassian, and Kurdish warriors trained from boyhood as elite cavalry) that became one of the most formidable military powers of the medieval Islamic world and the savior of Sunni Islam from two of its greatest threats: the Crusaders and the Mongols. The Mamluks overthrew the Ayyubid Dynasty and halted the [[event:91|Mongol]] advance at the [[event:2978|Battle of Ain Jalut]] (1260) — one of the first significant Mongol defeats — then systematically expelled the remaining Crusader states, culminating in the [[event:3531|fall of Acre]] in 1291.

The Mamluk system was a unique political institution: new sultans rose through military merit within the slave-soldier hierarchy, creating a meritocracy of violence where ability and faction-building determined advancement rather than heredity. This generated both military effectiveness (the Mamluk cavalry were among the finest heavy cavalry in the world) and chronic political instability (sultans were frequently assassinated by rival factions). Cairo under Mamluk rule became the cultural and religious capital of the Islamic world after [[event:2228|Baghdad's destruction]] — the Mamluks housed a puppet Abbasid Caliph, built the magnificent Sultan Hassan mosque-madrasa complex, and patronized Islamic scholarship, art, and architecture on a grand scale. The Mamluk Sultanate was eventually absorbed by the [[event:94|Ottoman Empire]] after Sultan Selim I's victory at the Battle of Marj Dabiq (1516) and conquest of Cairo in 1517, though the Mamluks continued as a governing class in Egypt under Ottoman suzerainty until Napoleon's invasion in 1798.
